are children born of two different families who have been joined by marriage. A male step-sibling is a **stepbrother** and a female is a **stepsister**. The step-siblings relationship is [connected through law](/wiki/Affinity_(law) "Affinity (law)") and is not a [blood relation](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Step-sister](/w/index.php?title=Step-sister&redirect=no "Step-sister"))

Child of a step-parent to whom one is not biologically related

For the 2018 film, see [Step Sisters](/wiki/Step_Sisters "Step Sisters"). For other uses, see [Step Brothers](/wiki/Step_Brothers_(disambiguation) "Step Brothers (disambiguation)").

| Charles and David are step-siblings as they are joined by the marriage of their parents Ben and Agatha. |

**Step-siblings** are children born of two different families who have been joined by marriage. A male step-sibling is a **stepbrother** and a female is a **stepsister**. The step-siblings relationship is [connected through law](/wiki/Affinity_(law) "Affinity (law)") and is not a [blood relation](/wiki/Consanguinity "Consanguinity").

Step-siblings are sometimes abbreviated informally as **stepsibs**.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stepsibling&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Culture")]

In many fairy tales, the central character has a stepmother and the step-siblings serve as an extension of their mother. [Cinderella](/wiki/Cinderella "Cinderella") and [Mother Hulda](/wiki/Mother_Hulda "Mother Hulda") features wicked stepsisters who take after their parents. The story *[Kate Crackernuts](/wiki/Kate_Crackernuts "Kate Crackernuts")* serves as a counterexample where the daughter of the evil stepparent is a loving stepsister.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Many [romance novels](/wiki/Romance_novel "Romance novel") feature heroes who are the stepbrother of the heroine. The step-relationship generally stems from a marriage when the hero and heroine are at least in their adolescence. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Some family [films](/wiki/Films "Films") and television [sitcoms](/wiki/Sitcoms "Sitcoms") feature a blended nuclear family including siblings as the center premise. In many cases, the step-family is large and full of children causing situations such as sibling rivalry, rooming, falling in love, and getting along amongst the children as popular plot-lines. This premise gained traction with the 1968 films *[Yours, Mine and Ours](/wiki/Yours,_Mine_and_Ours_(1968_film) "Yours, Mine and Ours (1968 film)")* and *[With Six You Get Eggroll](/wiki/With_Six_You_Get_Eggroll "With Six You Get Eggroll")* and the 1969 launch of the television sitcom *[The Brady Bunch](/wiki/The_Brady_Bunch "The Brady Bunch")*. Some contemporary family sitcoms have made the blended family sitcom more popular with the [TGIF](/wiki/TGIF_(ABC) "TGIF (ABC)") show *[Step by Step](/wiki/Step_by_Step_(TV_series) "Step by Step (TV series)")* bringing about other shows such as *[Aliens in the Family](/wiki/Aliens_in_the_Family "Aliens in the Family")*, *[Life with Derek](/wiki/Life_with_Derek "Life with Derek")*, *[Drake & Josh](/wiki/Drake_%26_Josh "Drake & Josh")*, and the short-lived [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC") family sitcom *[Something So Right](/wiki/Something_So_Right_(TV_series) "Something So Right (TV series)")*. The [Disney Channel](/wiki/Disney_Channel "Disney Channel") animated series *[Phineas and Ferb](/wiki/Phineas_and_Ferb "Phineas and Ferb")* also prominently features a blended family, chosen by co-creator [Jeff "Swampy" Marsh](/wiki/Jeff_%22Swampy%22_Marsh "Jeff \"Swampy\" Marsh") in part due to its under-use in children's programming, and his personal experiences growing up in such a family.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
